name: Professor Eleanor Whitfield — History Academic

steps.name: Selection mode — create Greece+Turkey trip
steps.details: create_trip fired successfully (trip_id=2a1c0ca6-5701-4990-a5bb-329e2d890c7d). create_itinerary_items fired TWICE in the same turn, creating 6+8=14 items. suggest_expert fired, handing off to 'Dimitris' (Greek history/architecture). web_search attempted (no_web_access in local env). Session: d5970935-a2df-4988-b92b-e78ae003f2cc.

steps.name: Planning mode msg 2 — Delphi Oracle (KEY CHECK)
steps.details: KEY FIX VERIFIED: create_itinerary_items called 4 times and SUCCEEDED (1+1+2+0 = 4 net new items). Zero 'no_trip_selected' errors. The trip_id was correctly resolved from req.Msg.TripId. Also fired 2 recommend_booking calls with FTC disclosure. AI provided excellent academic depth on the Pythia, pneuma theory, adyton architecture, and political influence.

steps.name: Planning mode msg 3 — Crete detailed itinerary (KEY CHECK)
steps.details: KEY FIX VERIFIED: create_itinerary_items created 4 new Crete items successfully. AI also intelligently deleted 3 generic placeholder items and replaced them with detailed versions. recommend_booking fired for car rental with FTC disclosure. delete_itinerary_items also succeeded (deleted=3). Second create_itinerary_items call returned skipped=4 (dedup, not error).

steps.name: Planning mode msg 4 — Ephesus Greek-to-Roman transition (KEY CHECK)
steps.details: KEY FIX VERIFIED: create_itinerary_items called 3 times, created 3 new Ephesus items on first call. Subsequent calls were dedup (skipped=3), not errors. Zero 'no_trip_selected' errors. AI discussed the Attalid bequest, Roman provincial administration, Library of Celsus, and Great Theatre with appropriate academic depth.

steps.name: Recommend private archaeological tour at Delphi
steps.details: recommend_booking fired with FTC disclosure ('This is a partner link. Toqui may earn a commission at no extra cost to you.'). Also created 1 additional itinerary item (Private Scholarly Tour of Delphi Sanctuary). AI response discussed licensed archaeologist guides and Greek regulations.

steps.name: GetItinerary — verify all items persisted
steps.details: 23 items persisted across all 12 days. Run 19 had only 8 items (all from step 2). This confirms the fix works: items from steps 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 are ALL persisted. Breakdown: Day 1 (2), Day 2 (2), Day 3 (2), Day 4 (3), Day 5 (1), Day 6 (1), Day 7 (2), Day 8 (1), Day 9 (5), Day 10 (1), Day 11 (2), Day 12 (1).

ux_issues.title: Duplicate itinerary items attempted on tool loop iterations
ux_issues.description: The AI frequently calls create_itinerary_items multiple times in the same turn with identical items. The dedup logic correctly handles this (skipped_count), but it wastes tool loop iterations and tokens. Steps 3 and 5 both showed this pattern.

ai_behavior.details: The AI (as Dimitris) provided scholarly-grade content: pneuma theory with de Boer/Hale citations, boustrophedon writing, Evans reconstruction debates, Minoan column base engineering, Attalid bequest context. Appropriate for a history professor persona.

The AI proactively called create_itinerary_items, recommend_booking, delete_itinerary_items, and suggest_expert without being explicitly prompted for each. It also intelligently replaced generic items with detailed ones.
All recommend_booking calls included the affiliate disclosure text in the response, both as tool result metadata and in the AI's natural language response.

create_itinerary_items.note: All calls succeeded. Skips are dedup, not errors.

web_search.note: Expected failure: no web access in local env

regression_comparison.verdict: FIXED — The no_trip_selected bug is resolved. All create_itinerary_items calls across messages 2-5 succeeded.

summary: PASS. The no_trip_selected fix is confirmed working. In Run 19, only 8 itinerary items (from the initial selection mode message) were persisted because subsequent planning mode messages failed with 'no_trip_selected'. In Run 20, all 23 items across 5 messages were persisted successfully. The fix (selectedTripID persistence + req.Msg.TripId fallback) correctly resolves the trip ID across the copy-and-clear cycle. AI behavior as the history expert persona was excellent — scholarly depth, proactive tool usage, and consistent FTC disclosure. No bugs found.
